title: Lesson 365
transcript: All right, so we just introduced the concept of remote access through VPNs. And I'll just remind you that a VPN really is a two-step process. requires tunneling first, which we defined as simply encapsulating one packet or datagram inside of another. And then if you want a VPN, it requires encryption on top of that. right so this is why especially when the industry refers to terms like tunneling and vpns we have to be careful and you have to be careful as far as the exam is concerned right because When the word VPN or the letters VPN are used, then that implies that you've got tunneling plus encryption. But you can also tunnel without encryption. And you might be wondering why do you want to do that? And the answer to that is really for two reasons. I mean tunneling is used first of all to force something to go down a specific path that is specified by the new header. So if you think about what tunneling is, we take a datagram that includes the original header and the data and we place it into the data portion of the new datagram then we're going to force that datagram that we've tunneled encapsulated regardless of what it had in its original header based on the new header that forces it to go down a specific path. And in fact, this is why it's called tunneling. We're forcing something to go down a specific path that is specified by the new header. But once you place something into that data portion, it's not automatically protected. It can still be seen. And that's what tunneling is. We're not protecting the data portion of the new datagram that contains, for example, the original packet or datagram. So the other reason why you want to do this is to carry across your network certain types of packets and datagrams that maybe cannot travel across those segments. And so they may be non-IP packets related to things like SCADA systems. It could be IPX or something like that, that you need to take and tunnel across for example packets that can travel across your network so that's the other reason why we want to do tunneling and so there are some protocols that appear specifically in the exam outline like GRE for example so GRE is a tunneling protocol that does not support encryption and therefore it cannot be used as a VPN but it can be used to tunnel for example non-IP packets across your IP network or we can use it to force something down a certain path as we said before so the animation here shows you what this really does but it does not support encryption so it cannot technically be used as a VPN because a VPN would be tunneling plus encryption All right, so that's what GRE, remember, GRE does not support encryption, can only tunnel, for example. Now, one of the other items that appears specifically in the exam outline is what's called split tunneling. If you think about these words, it tells you exactly what we're trying to do here. So let's imagine that you work for a company and you're traveling around, for example, and you check into your hotel you need to connect back in to your internal network obviously the your VPN solution that you have on your laptop is going to host that all right so let's say you connect to the hotel network and then you activate your VPN solution that'll create a a tunnel with encryption, because that's what a VPN is, right into your corporate network. Now let's say that you need to go out to the internet for whatever reason. The way that this would typically be hosted is obviously through your corporate network and the encryption that you're using to protect the traffic. What split tunneling allows is the bypassing of your corporate network and therefore the encryption and the policies that your organization may be imposing on you. So split tunneling solutions, what they allow you to do is even though you're connected, to your company's network through a VPN. It allows you, for example, to go out to the internet using the hotel network directly. And the reason that this really exists is to save bandwidth. But the problem from a security perspective is that now any policies that your organization may be imposing on you are going to be bypassed. And so really with split tunneling, you have to be careful. Because you do have a connection into your internal network, but now you also have an untrusted connection into the internet, bypassing your policies and therefore security controls that would normally have been imposed on by something that does not allow, for example, split tunneling. Thank you.
